facts: Nationalism, the belief that people with a shared identity should have their own nation, unified Germany and Italy in the 19th century because it inspired them to overthrow foreign rulers and merge independent states, not because of a single powerful king.

Imperialism refers to a stronger country taking control of a weaker one through military force or economic pressure; the British Empire used this to control India and parts of Africa, extracting resources like cotton and tea.

Colonialism is the practice of settling people from the imperial country in the colonized territory; the British in India built railroads to move resources but also imposed English language and laws, changing local culture.

The Scramble for Africa (c.1880–1910) saw European empires like Britain, France, Belgium, and Germany carve up Africa without regard for existing ethnic or political boundaries, as decided at the Berlin Conference of 1884–1885 where no African leaders were present.

Colonial extraction of resources often caused famines in colonized regions; for example, British policies in India prioritized exporting grain to Britain, leading to the Great Famine of 1876–1878 that killed millions.

Japan became an imperial power in the early 20th century by defeating Russia in the Russo-Japanese War (1904–1905) and later colonizing Korea and Taiwan, showing that imperialism was not limited to Western nations.
The United States also acted as an imperial power, taking control of the Philippines, Puerto Rico, and Guam after the Spanish-American War (1898), even though it claimed to support self-government.
misconceptions: Students often believe that imperialism only involved European countries and that non-European nations like Japan or the US never had empires, when in fact Japan colonized Korea and the US took the Philippines.

Students think the Berlin Conference was a meeting where African representatives agreed to European colonization, but actually no Africans were invited and Africa was divided solely by European powers.

Students believe that colonialism mainly aimed to help colonized peoples by bringing modern technology and education, ignoring that colonial powers extracted resources and caused famines, as seen in British India.
